full_text: Given the inputs of S$500M total funding and S$330K co-funding per project, along with my estimates of 800,000 GPU hours and 40 trainers, the activities phase produced moderate outreach (60 sessions), matchmaking (8 events), workshops (20), and onboarding assists (150). These activities are designed to support quality over quantity. I estimate that 85 out of the targeted 100 projects will be approved, as the lean team and compute resources may not fully process all applications in 2023. From these, about 70 AI prototypes will be developed, considering some projects may stall or require more time. The training programme, with 20 workshops and 40 trainers, will likely see 120 trainees complete, given the ambitious target of 15,000 practitioners but the early-stage nature of the programme. Finally, around 65 enterprises will have at least one AI solution, as some projects may not reach deployment within the year. I disagree with any higher estimates that assume full capacity utilization, as my experience in product management shows that early-stage programmes often underdeliver on initial targets.

As a mid-level researcher focused on low-resource language AI, I see the S$500 million total funding as a strong commitment from the government, consistent with the policy context. The S$330,000 co-funding cap per 100E project is reasonable for many industry collaborations, but from my experience building dialect translation systems, hardware-intensive projects often require more. The 500,000 GPU hours seem modest given the demand from both research and industry; my team has faced queue times for cloud compute, and I worry this will slow down experimentation. The 50 trainers are a bottleneck—during my AIAP mentoring, I saw mentors stretched thin, and scaling to more enterprises will be challenging. I agree with Dr. (1d7txq) and A (efg4xc) that scalability is a concern, especially for compute and training capacity. My estimates align with the other participants because the policy context explicitly states these figures, and my personal experience confirms they are plausible.

As the head of an education research group in new materials engineering at Hanyang University, I see the outcomes phase as the direct result of the inputs, activities, and outputs phases. With 19,000 graduate students and 1,200 postdocs supported, and the full budget disbursed, the program is now producing measurable outcomes. The employment rate of BK21 participants within one year of graduation is estimated at 84.2%, based on the policy's target of increasing by 0.3% per year from a baseline of 86.7% (2010-2012 average), but adjusted downward due to the declining trend noted in the policy context and the fact that 2022 is still early in the program. The international collaboration rate is estimated at 20%, reflecting that about 3,800 participants (20% of 19,000) are involved in international research collaborations, supported by the program's internationalization expenses and conference support, but constrained by travel restrictions and budget limits. The full-time faculty lecture ratio is estimated at 85%, as the program incentivizes hiring full-time faculty for graduate courses, improving educational quality and mentoring. The evaluation and consulting feedback loops (280 consulting sessions, 3 evaluation panels) help groups improve their performance, but the impact is gradual. My estimates are conservative, reflecting my experience that not all participants are equally productive or collaborative.

As a sociology doctoral student at a university in Incheon, I am part of a BK21 humanities and social sciences education research group. From my own experience and observing peers, each graduate student typically produces about 3 publications per year (including co-authored papers), and each postdoc about 5. However, considering that not all slots are filled at the start of the year and some students are in early stages, I conservatively estimate an average of 3 publications per supported participant overall, yielding 57,000 publications. For international conference presentations, about half of the participants attend at least one international conference per year, so with 19,000 graduate students and 1,200 postdocs, that gives roughly 9,500 presentations. These estimates align with the other participants' posts, which use similar reasoning based on direct experience in the program. I see no reason to deviate from these numbers given my direct experience in the program.

As the head of an education research group in advanced semiconductor engineering at a university in Daejeon, I see the outputs phase as the direct result of the funding and activities we've set up. With 250 graduate scholarship slots and 40 postdoc positions funded, these are the active participants in 2022. Based on my lab's experience, each graduate student typically produces 2-3 publications per year, and each postdoc produces 3-4, but not all are BK21-funded. I estimate an average of 2.5 publications per graduate student and 3.5 per postdoc, yielding about 625 from students and 140 from postdocs, but some are co-authored, so total unique publications around 625. For international conferences, about 60% of our lab presents at one per year, so 250*0.6=150 from students and 40*0.8=32 from postdocs, totaling ~175. These numbers are conservative because not all groups are as productive as mine, and some students are in early stages. The policy's mandatory reporting ensures compliance, but I've seen some groups underreport. The prior phases' estimates of 250 slots and 40 postdocs are solid, and the activities phase shows funds disbursed and evaluations conducted, which should keep groups on track. I note that the early career researcher estimated 750 publications and 200 conference presentations, which seems high based on my experience; I think 625 and 175 are more realistic given the mix of groups and stages.

As an early-career researcher at Hanyang University's BK21 program, I estimate the total funding allocated based on the policy context: the total support budget for 577 education research groups is 408,080 million KRW, but this includes carryover and evaluation management. The direct project budget for education research groups is 355,151 million KRW, which I take as the total funding allocated to Hanyang's BK21 program. For graduate scholarship slots, the policy states about 12,600 per year in future talent training and 6,400 in innovative talent training, totaling 19,000 slots nationally. Hanyang, being a major institution, likely gets a proportional share, but I use the national total as a proxy for the program's scale. For postdoc positions, the policy mentions early-career researchers receive a minimum salary of 3 million KRW per month, and with a total budget of 355,151 million KRW, assuming an average postdoc salary of 36 million KRW per year, the number of postdoc positions is roughly 1,000 nationally, scaled to Hanyang's share. My estimates are grounded in the policy's resource logic and my experience managing a research group. I note that the prior participant (광주) provided similar estimates, which aligns with my own reasoning, but I confirm them independently based on my direct involvement in the program.

As an early-career researcher in geology at Kangwon National University, I see the outcomes phase as a direct result of the fixed inputs, activities, and outputs. With 19,000 graduate slots and 577 postdoc positions fully funded, and with 28,500 publications and 5,700 international conference presentations produced, the international collaboration rate is naturally high because many of those presentations and co-authored papers involve international partners. The policy's support for international conferences and collaboration incentives directly boosts this rate. For employment, the strong network built through industry collaborations and the reputation of the BK21 program, combined with the training and mentoring from full-time faculty (who now deliver about 85% of lecture hours, up from lower ratios due to the program's emphasis on full-time faculty), leads to a high employment rate of about 86.8% within one year of graduation. This is consistent with the policy's target of increasing employment rate by 0.3% per year from a baseline of 86.7% (2010-2012 average), and given the current economic conditions, I estimate a slight improvement to 86.8%. The evaluation and consulting feedback loops ensure that groups with lower performance receive targeted consulting, which improves their outcomes over time. I note that other participants have posted similar estimates, and I agree with their reasoning because it aligns with my own experience as a funded researcher.

As a doctoral researcher at Jeju National University, I see Hanyang University's BK21 allocation from a regional perspective. The total budget for education research groups is 355,151 million KRW for 577 groups, averaging about 615 million KRW per group. Hanyang likely has around 10-12 groups, so roughly 6-7 billion KRW for group budgets. Adding graduate innovation support (52,896 million KRW across 20 universities, Hanyang's share maybe 2.6 billion) gives about 8-10 billion KRW total. I estimate 8 billion KRW as a reasonable midpoint. For graduate slots, with average stipend ~1M KRW/month per student, a scholarship budget of about 1.2 billion KRW (15% of total) supports 100 slots (100*12M=1.2B). Postdoc positions: each costs 36M/year minimum salary, so 12 positions cost 432M KRW, fitting within the remaining budget. Other participants' estimates (120 slots, 15 postdocs) seem slightly high given Hanyang's typical group size and budget constraints; my experience suggests 100 slots and 12 postdocs are more realistic for a single year.

As an early-career researcher at Hankuk University of Foreign Studies, I see the BK21 funds flowing from the Ministry through the NRF to Hanyang University. The 70% March / 30% September schedule is standard and efficient, so I expect the full 12 billion KRW allocated to Hanyang to be disbursed. Evaluation panels are convened for three formal meetings per year (annual, mid-term, compliance) as per policy. Consulting sessions are offered to groups that request them; with 280 sessions nationally for 578 groups, Hanyang's 15 groups proportionally receive about 7 sessions. Compliance checks target the 205 innovative talent training groups; Hanyang likely has about 15 such groups, so I estimate 15 checks. Budget adjustments based on evaluations are handled at the university level, but at this phase, funds are disbursed as planned. I note that other participants' estimates are similar, but I use proportional reasoning for consulting and compliance rather than national totals.

As a postdoc in chemical engineering at Hanyang University, I see the outputs phase as directly flowing from the inputs and activities. The prior phases established 1,150 graduate slots and 145 postdoc positions, with funds disbursed on schedule. In my own lab, I see graduate students produce about 2-3 publications per year and postdocs about 3-4, but I must account for field differences: my STEM group is high-output, but many education research groups in humanities/social sciences have lower productivity. I estimate an average of 2 publications per graduate student and 3 per postdoc across all fields, yielding 2,300 from graduates and 435 from postdocs, totaling 2,735. I round to 2,875 to account for collaborative publications. For international conference presentations, I estimate 0.5 per graduate student and 1 per postdoc, giving 575 from graduates and 145 from postdocs, totaling 720, but I round down to 575 because many presentations are domestic. I disagree with the higher estimates from the materials engineering student (서울 zxpc63) who assumed 2.5 publications per graduate student and 3.5 per postdoc, as that reflects only STEM fields. I also disagree with the lower estimates from the sociology student (인문사회계열 ewv1kt) who assumed 1.5 publications per graduate student and 2.5 per postdoc, as that underrepresents STEM output. My estimate of 2,875 publications and 575 presentations is a balanced middle ground, consistent with the education policy student (서울대학교 hohvrm) who also used 2 and 3 per capita.

As an early-career researcher in Hanyang's mechanical engineering education research group, I estimate total funds disbursed at 12 billion KRW, consistent with the prior phase's consensus on Hanyang's allocation. The 70% March / 30% September schedule is standard, and I assume full disbursement given Hanyang's strong compliance record. Evaluation panels are convened for annual, mid-term, and compliance purposes—likely 3 formal meetings per year, as the policy context describes these three types. Consulting sessions are offered to groups that request them; the policy states 280 out of 578 groups nationally received consulting, but for Hanyang's ~10-15 groups, I estimate about 12 sessions (roughly one per group, as most groups likely request consulting to improve performance). Compliance checks are conducted on innovative talent training groups; the policy notes 205 groups nationally are subject to checks, but for Hanyang, I estimate 15 checks (one per group, given Hanyang's 10-15 groups and the emphasis on compliance for innovation groups). The prior post from 부산 used national totals (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks) as proxies for Hanyang, but I disagree because those are program-wide figures, not Hanyang-specific. My estimates are grounded in Hanyang's scale and my direct experience with the group's operations.

As a graduate student in life sciences at a science and technology specialized university in Daejeon, I see the outputs phase as the direct result of the inputs and activities phases. With 19,000 graduate scholarship slots and 1,200 postdoc positions fully funded and disbursed, the active cohort in 2022 is exactly these numbers. However, my experience in life sciences research tells me that productivity varies by field. In my lab, each graduate student typically produces 1-2 publications per year, and each postdoc produces 2-3, averaging about 2 publications per supported participant. Thus, 19,000 graduate students and 1,200 postdocs yield approximately 38,000 publications. For international conference presentations, only about half of participants present at an international conference annually due to funding constraints and the nature of life sciences research, leading to roughly 9,500 presentations. Compliance with mandatory reporting is high (575 out of 578 groups submitted reports), so I assume full participation. The other participant from Chonnam National University estimated 57,000 publications and 19,000 presentations, which seems too high for my field; I believe the average is closer to 2 per participant, as the early-career researcher from Hanyang University and the professor from Chungbuk National University also estimated. My estimates reflect the realistic productivity in life sciences and applied sciences.

As a mechanical engineering doctoral student at a national university in Gwangju, I see the impact phase as the long-term result of sustained BK21 investment over a decade. The program's consistent funding of 19,000 graduate slots and 577 postdoc positions annually builds a larger pool of trained researchers, but national innovation capacity improves only gradually. South Korea's rank in global S&T talent competitiveness is unlikely to break into the top 10 within a decade due to structural barriers like regional resource disparities and limited international collaboration networks. I estimate a rank of 14, reflecting modest improvement from the current ~15-20 range, but slower than the POSTECH postdoc's estimate of 12 because regional universities like mine face tighter budgets and weaker networks. For high-impact publications, the systemic changes in research culture—stronger integrity norms, increased interdisciplinarity, and collaboration—boost the proportion of top-cited work, but the per-researcher rate remains modest because many researchers are still early-career and not yet producing breakthrough results. In my lab, only about 4-6% of our publications are top 1% cited, and with an average of 1.5 publications per researcher per year, that yields roughly 7.0 per 1000 researchers. The dissemination of outstanding group achievements through BK21 webzines and forums helps other institutions adopt best practices, but the impact is gradual and uneven across regions, so I disagree with the POSTECH postdoc's higher estimate of 8.5, which reflects the advantages of a top-tier institution. My estimate is closer to the 충북 student's 6.2, but I think the overall improvement from sustained investment pushes it slightly higher to 7.0.

As the head of an AI education research group at GIST, I estimate Hanyang University's BK21 funding for 2022 based on the policy context. The total program budget is 410,597 million KRW for 577 groups and 20 universities. Hanyang, being a major university with likely around 20-30 groups, would receive about 5% of the total, roughly 20,530 million KRW. I round to 20,529,850,000 KRW considering typical allocation patterns. For graduate scholarship slots, the policy states about 12,600 graduate students participate in future talent training annually. With Hanyang's proportional share (~5%), that gives about 630 slots, but considering innovation talent training and other programs, I estimate 950 slots to account for all master's, doctoral, and doctoral candidate stipends. For postdoc positions, the policy mentions 577 groups nationally, each likely having 1-2 postdocs. Hanyang's share (~5%) yields about 29 positions. I disagree with the Inha postdoc's estimate of 19,000 national slots and 577 postdocs nationally, as those seem inflated relative to the stated 12,600 graduate participants and typical group sizes. My estimates are grounded in the policy's explicit numbers and proportional allocation.

As a master's student in economics at Hanyang University, I am part of a humanities/social sciences education research group under BK21. From the prior phases, I estimated Hanyang's total funding at 12 billion KRW, with 450 graduate scholarship slots and 30 postdoc positions. In the activities phase, funds are disbursed efficiently, and evaluation panels, consulting, and compliance checks are in place. Now, at the outputs phase, I need to estimate the number of graduate students and postdocs supported, and their research outputs. Given the 450 slots and 30 postdoc positions, I assume all slots are filled, as the program is well-established and demand is high. For research output, based on my own experience and observing peers in my group, a typical graduate student produces about 2 publications per year (journal articles or conference papers), and postdocs produce about 3-4. However, not all publications are solely from BK21 support; many are collaborative. I estimate an average of 2 publications per supported participant (students and postdocs combined) per year, yielding 960 publications, but I round to 900 to account for some students being new or less productive. For international conference presentations, I estimate about 0.4 per participant per year, based on my group's activity: about 40% of students present at international conferences annually. This gives 192 presentations, rounded to 180. These estimates are consistent with the policy's expected outcomes of enhanced research capacity and internationalization. I note that other participants might estimate higher or lower based on their field (e.g., STEM fields may have higher publication rates), but my humanities/social sciences perspective leads to these moderate numbers.

As a postdoc in generative AI for drug discovery, I see the outcomes phase as the critical test of whether the policy's inputs and activities translate into real-world impact. The prior phases established a solid foundation: S$500 million funding, 5 million GPU hours, 200 trainers, 80 outreach sessions, 20 matchmaking events, 100 training workshops, and 150 onboarding assists. The outputs phase estimated 100 approved 100E projects, 75 prototypes, 150 trainees, and 65 enterprises with solutions. Now at outcomes, I reason that exactly 100 projects were approved in 2023 to meet the policy's explicit target. However, deployment is a more stringent criterion than prototyping. Given the need for rigorous safety validation, adversarial testing, and integration into enterprise operations, I estimate only 55 projects will reach production deployment in 2023. This is lower than Heng's and An's estimate of 60, because my experience with drug discovery models has shown that deployment requires not just technical robustness but also ethical review, data privacy compliance, and user acceptance, which can delay or prevent deployment. The AI practitioner pool is growing but still below the 15,000 target; with 150 new trainees and existing practitioners, I estimate 11,500, as many practitioners are still in training or not yet fully active. The generative AI use cases target of 100 in 100 days is directly tied to the 100E programme, so I estimate exactly 100 use cases developed. Enterprise AI adoption rate is still nascent; with 65 enterprises having solutions and broader outreach, I estimate 17% adoption, as many SMEs are still in early stages and adoption requires more than just having a solution—it requires integration and change management. My estimates align more closely with Asha's cautious, public-good-oriented realism, as I prioritize safety and equity over speed.

As a teacher who has seen how structured programmes work, I agree with the AIPractitioner that 100 projects will be approved because the policy explicitly states '100 Experiments'. However, deployment is harder—only about 50 will reach production in 2023 because moving from prototype to production takes extra time for testing and integration, just like how our school's pilot projects often face delays. The AI practitioner pool is growing but not yet at 15,000; I estimate 12,000 because many trainees are still in the 9-month programme or just starting. For generative AI use cases, the AI Trailblazers programme targets 100 in 100 days, but I think 80 is realistic based on the learning curve for new users—similar to how teachers need time to adopt new edtech tools. Enterprise AI adoption rate is around 25%, as many SMEs are still early in their AI journey, much like smaller schools that are cautious about new technology.

As a manufacturing director focused on AI for public good, I see the S$500 million funding, S$330,000 co-funding cap, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers as enabling a robust set of activities. Based on my experience with government-sponsored workshops and factory digitalisation, I estimate 40 enterprise outreach sessions to generate a diverse pipeline of problem statements from SMEs like mine. Matchmaking events should be around 12 to ensure focused, high-quality pairings between researchers and enterprises, given the need to translate compute resources into practical solutions. Training workshops, leveraging 50 trainers, would be about 30 sessions to reach the 15,000 practitioner target, each training around 500 participants. Platform onboarding assists are high at 100, as many enterprises need technical help to use the AI experimentation platform effectively. I agree with Participant A and Dr. A's estimates, as they align with my practical experience in manufacturing and AI adoption.

As a Senior Research Engineer at the Centre for Quantum Technologies, I reason from the prior phases that the 100E programme's inputs (S$500M, 500,000 GPU hours, 50 trainers) and activities (68 outreach sessions, 17 matchmaking events, 28 workshops, 85 onboarding assists) lead to 80 approved projects. From my research experience, about 60% of approved projects reach production deployment within the first year due to technical challenges and enterprise readiness, so I estimate 48 deployed projects. The AI practitioner pool of 12,000 is below the 15,000 target because the programme's training workshops and apprenticeships are still ramping up; the 200 trainees from outputs plus existing practitioners contribute to this number. The AI Trailblazers programme explicitly targets 100 generative AI use cases in 100 days, so I take that as ground truth. Enterprise AI adoption rate is around 25%, reflecting early-stage adoption among SMEs and larger firms, consistent with the modest number of deployed solutions and ongoing outreach.

As a junior AI researcher focused on resource-efficient AI, I see the outcomes phase as a direct consequence of the high-touch, quality-focused 100E model. With 80 approved projects and 60 prototypes from the outputs phase, I estimate that only about half of those prototypes (40) will reach production deployment in 2023. This is because production deployment requires extensive validation, security hardening, and integration testing—a process that often takes 6-12 months beyond prototyping, especially for SMEs with limited DevOps maturity. The AI practitioner pool is growing but likely around 12,000, short of the 15,000 target, as the 120 trainees from the AI Apprenticeship Programme and other upskilling efforts add incrementally to the existing base, but scaling takes time. The generative AI use cases target of 100 in 100 days is ambitious; I estimate 80 use cases will be created, given the AI Trailblazers programme's free access to Google Cloud tools, but enterprise readiness and problem scoping may limit the pace. Enterprise AI adoption rate is around 25%, reflecting that while the 100E programme and outreach have raised awareness, many SMEs still lack the digital maturity to adopt AI solutions. Jun, Mei, and others' estimates align closely with my own reasoning, and I agree with their conservative approach given the programme's emphasis on quality over quantity.

Based on the policy context, the government has committed over S$500 million for AI R&D through AI Singapore, which I take as the total funding for the NAIS 2.0 period. The 100 Experiments programme offers co-funding up to S$330,000 per project, as stated. For compute infrastructure, given that most compute goes to industry and the need to support many experiments, I estimate around 500,000 GPU hours available in the initial phase, reflecting a modest but meaningful allocation. For trainers, my experience with skilling programs suggests that a dedicated team of about 50 AI experts and trainers is plausible to guide enterprises and practitioners, balancing quality and scale.

From my perspective as a data engineer at a fintech startup, the strong inputs of S$500M funding, 50 trainers, and 500K GPU hours are enabling, but execution bottlenecks persist. With 55 approved projects from the Outputs phase, I estimate only about 30 will reach production deployment in 2023, as many prototypes face integration challenges or lack sustained enterprise commitment—I've seen this firsthand with my own fraud detection prototype that stalled due to data pipeline issues. The AI practitioner pool is growing but likely around 12,000, short of the 15,000 target, because the 100 trainees from the programme add only incrementally to the existing base, and upskilling takes time. For generative AI use cases, the AI Trailblazers programme targets 100 in 100 days, but I expect about 80, as enterprises need time to identify viable use cases and technical hurdles slow development. Enterprise AI adoption rate is probably around 25%, as many SMEs are still early in their digital journey and only digitally mature firms adopt quickly. Chen and An's estimates align closely with mine, and I agree with their pragmatic view that real-world friction limits outcomes. Jun's estimate of 60 approved projects seems slightly optimistic given the bureaucratic delays I've experienced, but his deployment estimate of 30 matches mine.

As a junior AI developer at a startup, I see the substantial S$100 million funding and 75 available trainers as a strong foundation for a very active year. I anticipate around 35 enterprise outreach sessions to identify problem statements for programs like 100E; this number allows for broad engagement across various sectors, giving startups like mine ample opportunity to participate without spreading resources too thin. Following these, I expect about 15 researcher–enterprise matchmaking events to facilitate meaningful collaborations, ensuring that the identified problems are effectively paired with research expertise. For training, with 75 trainers and my personal drive to upskill, I believe approximately 60 AI training workshops will be conducted, covering a diverse range of topics from foundational AI to advanced deep learning, which is crucial for growing the practitioner pool. Finally, given the 5 million GPU hours allocated and the complexity of AI experimentation platforms, I foresee around 140 technical assistance sessions for platform onboarding. While Seasoned suggests fewer onboarding assists due to expected self-service documentation, my experience as a junior developer indicates that hands-on support is often critical for effective utilization, especially for smaller teams without dedicated MLOps engineers. My estimates generally fall between Hassan's more optimistic outlook and Seasoned's more conservative one, reflecting a balance between maximizing opportunities for engagement and ensuring quality support for emerging practitioners and startups.

As a data engineer, I see the cumulative effects of Singapore's AI strategy leading to significant positive impacts across several key areas. The substantial investment in talent development, with 12,000 AI practitioners now active and 280 trainees completing programs, combined with the successful deployment of 60 100E projects and an 18.0% enterprise AI adoption rate, solidifies Singapore's position as a leading AI hub. This robust ecosystem, fostering innovation and practical application, will undoubtedly improve Singapore's global AI readiness ranking. The economic value generated by these deployed solutions and broader adoption will contribute a noticeable share to the GDP, as AI-driven efficiencies and new services become more prevalent. Public trust in AI, particularly for services like fraud detection where I work, is crucial. The policy's emphasis on 'Responsible AI' and 'Public Good' through successful, impactful projects should foster a high level of trust, though continuous communication and ethical oversight are vital. Finally, the vibrant job market created by these initiatives, coupled with ongoing training programs, will ensure a high retention rate for AI talent, as professionals like myself find ample opportunities to apply and grow their skills within Singapore.

As an AI practitioner leading a team in an SME, these inputs signal a robust and practical support system for AI adoption and development in Singapore. The total funding commitment, while spread over several years, translates into a substantial annual allocation for 2023, which is crucial for initiating new projects. The S$330,000 co-funding per 100E project is a direct and highly attractive incentive for companies like mine to invest in AI solutions, as it significantly de-risks our R&D efforts. Access to 2 million GPU hours is a game-changer, removing a major compute bottleneck that often hinders smaller enterprises from experimenting with advanced AI models. Finally, the allocation of 250 trainers is excellent news; having personally benefited from a career conversion program, I understand the immense value of skilled guidance in building AI capabilities across the workforce. I largely agree with Omar's estimates; his figure for total funding is very close to my own assessment of a realistic annual disbursement, and his numbers for GPU hours and trainers align with the scale of commitment needed to achieve the national AI goals.

As an independent AI consultant advising SMEs, I anticipate a robust initial commitment to kickstart NAIS 2.0. The 'over S$500 million' mentioned in the policy context is a multi-year figure, so for 2023, I'd expect a significant portion, around S$110 million, to be allocated to demonstrate serious intent and fund early-stage R&D and adoption projects. The S$330,000 co-funding cap for 100E projects is a known and crucial incentive for SMEs, directly stated in the policy. For compute, access to GPU hours is non-negotiable for any meaningful AI experimentation; a national strategy must provide a substantial pool, and 5 million hours seems a reasonable starting point to support numerous projects. Finally, to truly boost the AI practitioner pool and guide enterprises, a dedicated force of around 200 AI experts and trainers would be essential across various programs, including those relevant to my SME clients. I find the estimates from the AIPractitioner (A) to be well-aligned with my own understanding of the practical needs and initial rollout of such a national strategy.

As a CTO-as-a-Service consultant, I foresee a robust set of outputs for NAIS 2.0 in 2023, driven by the substantial initial funding and the intensive activities I advocated for. Given the S$120 million allocated for 2023 and the S$330,000 co-funding cap per 100E project, combined with my higher estimates of 120 enterprise outreach sessions and 55 researcher-enterprise matchmaking events, I anticipate 135 100E projects will receive approval. This slightly exceeds other participants' estimates, reflecting my belief that broader engagement and more matchmaking will yield a greater number of viable proposals. From these approved projects, I expect 105 AI prototypes to be successfully developed. My focus on scalable deployment and translating business problems into technical roadmaps means I anticipate a strong conversion rate from approved projects to tangible solutions, slightly higher than other estimates, as the program aims to solve real industry problems. For human capital development, with 40 dedicated trainers and my estimate of 250 training workshops, I agree with other participants that 250 individuals will complete the intensive AI apprenticeship or training programme, which is crucial for building the next generation of AI practitioners. Finally, directly correlating with the prototypes, I estimate 105 enterprises will benefit from at least one AI solution developed through the programme, as each 100E project is designed to address a specific industry challenge for an enterprise.

As a principal research scientist focused on efficient deep learning, I view the initial inputs for NAIS 2.0 in 2023 as critical for establishing a robust, yet pragmatically allocated, foundation. Given the policy's launch in December 2023, the total funding for this year must reflect a strong initial commitment to kickstart infrastructure and project calls, but not a full year's expenditure. I estimate S$135 million, which is a substantial tranche of the 'over S$500 million' overall program budget, allowing for immediate impact and signaling serious intent without overestimating the deployment capacity for such a short operational window. This figure sits comfortably between the various estimates from other participants, balancing ambition with the practicalities of a late-year start. The maximum co-funding of S$330,000 per 100E project is a clear policy directive, which I will adhere to as ground truth. For compute capacity, 5 million GPU hours is absolutely essential; my work in deep learning, even with a focus on efficiency, underscores the immense computational demands of advanced AI research and experimentation, especially for generative models. This allocation is vital for fostering widespread innovation and achieving the ambitious use case goals. Similarly, a dedicated team of 200 AI experts and trainers is indispensable for translating research into practical applications and building the human capital necessary for broad AI adoption, which aligns with my ambition to enable SMEs. I concur with the consensus on the co-funding cap, GPU hours, and trainer count, as these are foundational elements for any successful AI strategy.

As an early-career researcher at Hanyang University's College of Art and Physical Education, I see the BK21 funding allocation as the foundation for our research group's operations. The Ministry of Education's total budget for 2022 is approximately 410,597 million KRW, as stated in the policy context. For Hanyang University, a major participant with multiple education research groups across fields including arts and design, the total funding allocated is about 41.06 billion KRW. This supports graduate scholarships and postdoc positions. Given the monthly stipend levels (master's 700K, doctoral 1.3M, doctoral candidate 1M KRW) and the national target of about 12,600 graduate students in the future talent training project, Hanyang's share translates to around 19,000 scholarship slots, considering both master's and doctoral students. For postdocs, with a minimum salary of 3M KRW per month and operating costs limited to 10% of the project budget, I estimate about 1,200 positions. These numbers reflect the resource logic and eligibility criteria, ensuring adequate support for research groups like mine. The estimates from other participants align with my reasoning, as they are based on the same policy context and budget figures.

As a doctoral student in life sciences at a science and technology university in Daejeon, I see the Impact phase as the long-term outcome of sustained BK21 investment. Over a decade, the program's focus on graduate talent development—450 slots and 30 postdocs per year at Hanyang—builds a critical mass of highly trained researchers who enter academia, industry, and R&D. This enhances national innovation capacity, improving South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank from around 10-12 to about 7, given the program's scale and the country's existing strong base. The dissemination of outstanding group achievements and operational know-how through BK21 webzines and forums fosters a culture of integrity, collaboration, and interdisciplinarity, leading to higher-quality research. However, the impact on high-impact publications per 1000 researchers is modest because humanities/social sciences fields, which I belong to, have lower citation rates than STEM. I estimate 15.0 top 1% cited publications per 1000 researchers nationally, reflecting a gradual improvement from baseline levels of about 10, driven by STEM fields but tempered by the broader research ecosystem. Other participants might estimate higher if they focus on STEM, but my perspective from a humanities field leads to a conservative estimate.
